risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The allocation of the participants to the study arms was done centrally by block randomization (block size of 6) stratified by country ... The Pharmacy Department of the Hospital Clinic in Barcelona produced and safeguarded the computer-generated randomization list for each recruiting site until unblinding" Comment: This clearly describes a proper random allocation process using a computer-generated randomization list, with block randomization and stratification, which are consistent with a truly random sequence.

Quote: "The allocation of the participants to the study arms was done centrally by block randomization (block size of 6) stratified by country... The Pharmacy Department of the Hospital Clinic in Barcelona produced and safeguarded the computer-generated randomization list for each recruiting site until unblinding, and carried out the masking, labelling, and packaging of all study interventional drugs. Study number allocation for each participant was concealed in opaque sealed envelopes that were sequentially numbered and opened only after recruitment by study health personnel." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment: central allocation by the pharmacy plus sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes opened only after recruitment. That meets the criterion for low risk of allocation concealment bias.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An individually randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial" and "Investigators, laboratory staff, care providers, and study participants were blinded to intervention throughout the study." Comment: The study explicitly states that both participants and care providers/personnel were blinded, with placebo tablets identical to mefloquine. Although some tolerability outcomes differed between groups, the main clinical and parasitological outcomes are not outcomes that would be expected to be systematically driven by knowledge of allocation, and the reported masking makes performance bias for the study overall unlikely.

Quote: "Investigators, laboratory staff, care providers, and study participants were blinded to intervention throughout the study." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by blinded personnel, and the key efficacy outcomes were largely objective/laboratory-based (e.g. microscopy, PCR, Hb, viral load), so knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to influence measurement.

Quote: "The main analysis was done on the Intention-to-Treat (ITT) cohort that included all recruited women that met the inclusion criteria and had data on the specific outcomes, and on the According to Protocol (ATP) cohort that included all women who had received three doses of IPTp according to the pre-specified schedule, and have results available on peripheral parasitemia at delivery." Comment: Although there are outcome-specific missing data (e.g., delivery peripheral blood samples were available for 490/537 control and 483/534 MQ; placenta infection N=462/449; viral load had 'No data' for 70/73), the attrition appears moderate and broadly balanced between groups, with the paper reporting ITT analyses and similar baseline characteristics. The trial was powered allowing for 15% losses to follow-up, and the observed missingness is around this level for key outcomes, with no clear signal that exclusions or missing data were differential in a way likely to bias results.
